note: When a core Linux tool (git, ssh, curl, etc.) is corrupted or misbehaving, apt-get install --reinstall <pkg> (or dpkg --configure -a) often fully restores it. Diagnose first: <tool> --version, ls -l $(which <tool>), dpkg -l <pkg> | grep ^ii, then reinstall. Use DEBIAN_FRONTEND=noninteractive apt-get -y ... to avoid prompts.

Terminal task loop: (1) read task prompt fully, (2) capture current state with non-destructive commands (cat, ls, which, --version), (3) form a minimal hypothesis, (4) apply the smallest fix, (5) verify using the same command that originally exposed the issue, (6) submit DONE only after verification passes.

Each bash() call is independent — no cwd/env persists. Chain with && or ; when ordering matters, and export vars inline. Prefer one compact command chain per call over many tiny calls to save round-trips.
